title: Flute

infobox.value: is a member of a family of musical instruments in the [woodwind](/wiki/Woodwind "Woodwind") group. Like all woodwinds, flutes are [aerophones](/wiki/Aerophones "Aerophones"), producing sound with a vibrating column of air. Flutes produce sound when the player's air flows across an opening. In the [Hornbostel–Sachs](/wiki/Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs "Hornbostel–Sachs") classification system, flutes are [edge-blown aerophones](/wiki/Edge-blown_aerophones "Edge-blown aerophones").[[1]](#cite_note-1) A musician who plays the flute is called a **flautist** or **flutist**.

Woodwind instrument

The **flute** is a member of a family of musical instruments in the [woodwind](/wiki/Woodwind "Woodwind") group. Like all woodwinds, flutes are [aerophones](/wiki/Aerophones "Aerophones"), producing sound with a vibrating column of air. Flutes produce sound when the player's air flows across an opening. In the [Hornbostel–Sachs](/wiki/Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs "Hornbostel–Sachs") classification system, flutes are [edge-blown aerophones](/wiki/Edge-blown_aerophones "Edge-blown aerophones").[[1]](#cite_note-1) A musician who plays the flute is called a **flautist** or **flutist**.

[Paleolithic flutes](/wiki/Paleolithic_flute "Paleolithic flute") with hand-bored holes are the earliest known identifiable musical instruments. A number of flutes dating to about 53,000 to 45,000 years ago have been found in the [Swabian Jura](/wiki/Swabian_Jura "Swabian Jura") region of present-day [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), indicating a developed musical tradition from the earliest period of modern human presence in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe").[[2]](#cite_note-NYTimes-2)[[3]](#cite_note-jhevol-3) While the oldest flutes currently known were found in Europe, Asia also has a long history with the instrument. A playable bone flute discovered in China is dated to about 9,000 years ago.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The Americas also had an ancient flute culture, with instruments found in [Caral](/wiki/Caral "Caral"), [Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru"), dating back 5,000 years [[5]](#cite_note-5) and in [Labrador](/wiki/Labrador "Labrador") dating back about 7,500 years.[[6]](#cite_note-Goss_2019_dev_flutes_northamerica-6)

The [bamboo flute](/wiki/Bamboo_flute "Bamboo flute") has a long history, especially in China and India. Flutes have been discovered in historical records and artworks starting in the [Zhou dynasty](/wiki/Zhou_dynasty "Zhou dynasty") (c.1046–256 BC). The oldest written sources reveal the Chinese were using the [kuan](/wiki/Guan_(instrument) "Guan (instrument)") (a reed instrument) and [xīao](/wiki/Xiao_(flute) "Xiao (flute)") (an [end-blown flute](/wiki/End-blown_flute "End-blown flute"), often of bamboo) in the 12th–11th centuries BC, followed by the chi (or ch'ih) in the 9th century BC and the yüeh in the 8th century BC.[[7]](#cite_note-sachs1-7) Of these, the bamboo chi is the oldest documented [transverse flute](/wiki/Transverse_flute "Transverse flute").[[7]](#cite_note-sachs1-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Musicologist [Curt Sachs](/wiki/Curt_Sachs "Curt Sachs") called the cross flute (Sanskrit: vāṃśī) "the outstanding wind instrument of ancient India", and said that religious artwork depicting "celestial music" instruments was linked to music with an "aristocratic character".[[9]](#cite_note-sachs2-9) The Indian bamboo cross flute, [Bansuri](/wiki/Bansuri "Bansuri"), was sacred to [Krishna](/wiki/Krishna "Krishna"), who is depicted with the instrument in [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") art.[[9]](#cite_note-sachs2-9) In India, the [cross flute](/wiki/Transverse_flute "Transverse flute") appeared in reliefs from the 1st century AD at [Sanchi](/wiki/Sanchi "Sanchi") and [Amaravati](/wiki/Amaravati#History "Amaravati") from the 2nd–4th centuries AD.[[9]](#cite_note-sachs2-9)[[10]](#cite_note-MusINstrNepal-10)

According to historian Alexander Buchner, there were flutes in Europe in prehistoric times, but they disappeared from the continent until flutes arrived from Asia by way of "North Africa, Hungary, and Bohemia".[[11]](#cite_note-pichistory-11) The end-blown flute began to be seen in illustration in the 11th century.[[11]](#cite_note-pichistory-11) Transverse flutes entered Europe through [Byzantium](/wiki/Byzantium "Byzantium") and were depicted in Greek art about 800 AD.[[12]](#cite_note-sachs3-12) The transverse flute had spread into Europe by way of Germany, and was known as the German flute.[[12]](#cite_note-sachs3-12)

## Etymology and terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flute&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology and terminology")]

The word *flute* first appeared in the English language during the [Middle English](/wiki/Middle_English "Middle English") period, as *floute*,[[13]](#cite_note-Flute-13) *flowte*, or *flo(y)te*,[[14]](#cite_note-C_Weiner_1989-14) possibly from [Old French](/wiki/Old_French "Old French") *flaute* and [Old Provençal](/wiki/Old_Proven%C3%A7al "Old Provençal") *flaüt*,[[13]](#cite_note-Flute-13) or possibly from Old French *fleüte*, *flaüte*, *flahute* via [Middle High German](/wiki/Middle_High_German "Middle High German") *floite* or [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language") *fluit*. The English verb *flout* has the same linguistic root, and the modern Dutch verb *fluiten* still shares the two meanings.[[15]](#cite_note-Fenwick-15) Attempts to trace the word back to the Latin *flare* (to blow, inflate) have been called "phonologically impossible" or "inadmissable".[[14]](#cite_note-C_Weiner_1989-14) The first known use of the word *flute* was in the 14th century.[[16]](#cite_note-16) According to the *Oxford English Dictionary*, this was in [Geoffrey Chaucer](/wiki/Geoffrey_Chaucer "Geoffrey Chaucer")'s *[The Hous of Fame](/wiki/The_Hous_of_Fame "The Hous of Fame")*, c. 1380.[[14]](#cite_note-C_Weiner_1989-14)

A musician who plays any instrument in the flute family can be called a flutist,[[17]](#cite_note-17) flautist,[[18]](#cite_note-18) or flute player. *Flutist* dates back to at least 1603, the earliest quotation cited by the *Oxford English Dictionary*. *Flautist* was used in 1860 by [Nathaniel Hawthorne](/wiki/Nathaniel_Hawthorne "Nathaniel Hawthorne") in *[The Marble Faun](/wiki/The_Marble_Faun "The Marble Faun")*, after being adopted during the 18th century from Italy (*flautista*, itself from *flauto*), like many musical terms in England since the [Italian Renaissance](/wiki/Italian_Renaissance "Italian Renaissance"). Other English terms, now virtually obsolete, are *fluter* (15th–19th centuries)[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) and *flutenist* (17th and 18th centuries).[[15]](#cite_note-Fenwick-15)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flute&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Further information: [Paleolithic flutes](/wiki/Paleolithic_flutes "Paleolithic flutes") and [Prehistoric music](/wiki/Prehistoric_music "Prehistoric music")

[![12th-century art, Chinese women playing flutes](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Gu_Hongzhong%27s_Night_Revels%2C_Detail_4.jpg/250px-Gu_Hongzhong%27s_Night_Revels%2C_Detail_4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gu_Hongzhong%27s_Night_Revels,_Detail_4.jpg)

Chinese women playing flutes, from the 12th-century [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty") remake of the *Night Revels of Han Xizai*, originally by [Gu Hongzhong](/wiki/Gu_Hongzhong "Gu Hongzhong") (10th century)

A fragment of a juvenile [cave bear](/wiki/Cave_bear "Cave bear")'s [femur](/wiki/Femur "Femur"), with two to four holes, was found at [Divje Babe](/wiki/Divje_Babe_flute "Divje Babe flute") in [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia") and dated to about 43,000 years ago. It may be the oldest flute discovered, but this has been disputed.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) In 2008, a flute dated to at least 35,000 years ago was discovered in [Hohle Fels](/wiki/Hohle_Fels "Hohle Fels") cave near [Ulm](/wiki/Ulm "Ulm"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany").[[25]](#cite_note-25) It is a five-holed flute with a V-shaped mouthpiece and was made from a [vulture](/wiki/Vulture "Vulture") wing bone. The discovery was published in the journal *[Nature](/wiki/Nature_(journal) "Nature (journal)")*, in August 2009.[[26]](#cite_note-26) This was the oldest confirmed musical instrument ever found,[[27]](#cite_note-BBC-27) until a redating of flutes found in [Geißenklösterle](/wiki/Gei%C3%9Fenkl%C3%B6sterle "Geißenklösterle") cave revealed them to be older, at 42,000 to 43,000 years.[[3]](#cite_note-jhevol-3)

The Hohle Fels flute is one of several found in the [Hohle Fels cavern](/wiki/Hohle_Fels "Hohle Fels") next to the [Venus of Hohle Fels](/wiki/Venus_of_Hohle_Fels "Venus of Hohle Fels") and a short distance from the oldest known human carving.[[28]](#cite_note-28) On announcing the discovery, scientists suggested that the "finds demonstrate the presence of a well-established musical tradition at the time when modern humans colonized Europe".[[29]](#cite_note-29) Scientists have also suggested that this flute's discovery may help to explain "the probable behavioural and cognitive gulf between" [Neanderthals](/wiki/Neanderthals "Neanderthals") and [early modern human](/wiki/Early_modern_humans "Early modern humans").[[27]](#cite_note-BBC-27)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/A_medieval_bone_flute_dating_11th-13th_century._%28FindID_621082%29.jpg/250px-A_medieval_bone_flute_dating_11th-13th_century._%28FindID_621082%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_medieval_bone_flute_dating_11th-13th_century._(FindID_621082).jpg)

Bone flute made of a goat's tibia, 11th–13th century AD.

An 18.7 cm flute with three holes, made from a [mammoth](/wiki/Mammoth "Mammoth") tusk and dated to 30,000–37,000 years ago, was found in 2004 in the [Geißenklösterle](/wiki/Gei%C3%9Fenkl%C3%B6sterle "Geißenklösterle") cave near Ulm, in the southern German [Swabian Alb](/wiki/Swabian_Alb "Swabian Alb").[[30]](#cite_note-30) Two flutes made from [swan](/wiki/Swan "Swan") bones were excavated a decade earlier from the same cave and dated to about 36,000 years ago.

A playable 9,000-year-old Chinese [Gudi](/wiki/Gudi_(instrument) "Gudi (instrument)") (literally, "bone flute") was excavated from a tomb in [Jiahu](/wiki/Jiahu "Jiahu") along with 29 similar specimens.[[31]](#cite_note-31) They were made from the wing bones of red-crowned cranes and each has five to eight holes.[[32]](#cite_note-32)
The earliest extant Chinese transverse flute is a *chi* ([篪](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%AF%AA "wiktionary:篪")) flute discovered in the [Tomb of Marquis Yi of Zeng](/wiki/Tomb_of_Marquis_Yi_of_Zeng "Tomb of Marquis Yi of Zeng") at the [Suizhou](/wiki/Suizhou "Suizhou") site, [Hubei](/wiki/Hubei "Hubei") province, [China](/wiki/China "China"), dating from 433 BC, during the later [Zhou dynasty](/wiki/Zhou_dynasty "Zhou dynasty").[[33]](#cite_note-33) It is fashioned of [lacquered](/wiki/Lacquer "Lacquer") bamboo with closed ends and has five stops on the flute's side instead of the top. *[Shi Jing](/wiki/Shi_Jing "Shi Jing")*, traditionally said to have been compiled and edited by [Confucius](/wiki/Confucius "Confucius"), mentions chi flutes.

The earliest written reference to a flute is from a [Sumerian](/wiki/Sumerian_language "Sumerian language")-language [cuneiform tablet](/wiki/Cuneiform "Cuneiform") dated to c. 2600–2700 BC.[[34]](#cite_note-34)
Flutes are mentioned in a recently translated tablet of the [Epic of Gilgamesh](/wiki/Gilgamesh "Gilgamesh"), an epic poem whose development spanned the period from about 2100–600 BC.[[35]](#cite_note-Meso_Flutes-35) A set of cuneiform tablets knows as the "*musical texts*" provide precise tuning instructions for seven scales of a stringed instrument (assumed to be a Babylonian [lyre](/wiki/Lyre "Lyre")). One of those scales is named "*embūbum*", which is an [Akkadian](/wiki/Akkadian_language "Akkadian language") word for "flute".[[35]](#cite_note-Meso_Flutes-35)

[The Bible](/wiki/The_Bible "The Bible"), in [Genesis](/wiki/Book_of_Genesis "Book of Genesis") 4:21, cites [Jubal](/wiki/Jubal_(Bible) "Jubal (Bible)") as being the "father of all those who play the *ugab* and the *[kinnor](/wiki/Kinnor "Kinnor")*". The former Hebrew term is believed by some to refer to a wind instrument, or wind instruments in general, the latter to a stringed instrument, or stringed instruments in general. As such, Jubal is regarded in the Judeo-Christian tradition as the inventor of the flute (a word used in some translations of this biblical passage).[[36]](#cite_note-braun2004-36) In other sections of the Bible (1 [Samuel](/wiki/Samuel_book "Samuel book") 10:5, 1 [Kings](/wiki/Books_of_Kings "Books of Kings") 1:40, [Isaiah](/wiki/Book_of_Isaiah "Book of Isaiah") 5:12 and 30:29, and [Jeremiah](/wiki/Book_of_Jeremiah "Book of Jeremiah") 48:36) the flute is referred to as "*chalil*", from the root word for "hollow".[[37]](#cite_note-37) Archeological digs in the Holy Land have discovered flutes from the Bronze Age (c. 4000–1200 BC) and the Iron Age (1200–586 BC), the latter era "witness[ing] the creation of the Israelite kingdom and its separation into the two kingdoms of Israel and Judea."[[36]](#cite_note-braun2004-36)

Some early flutes were made out of [tibias](/wiki/Tibia "Tibia") (shin bones). The flute has also always been an essential part of [Indian culture](/wiki/Indian_culture "Indian culture"),[[38]](#cite_note-38) and the cross flute believed by several accounts to originate in [India](/wiki/India "India")[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40) as Indian literature from 1500 BC has made vague references to the cross flute.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

## Acoustics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flute&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Acoustics")]

A flute produces sound when a stream of air directed across a hole in the instrument creates a vibration of air at the hole.[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43) The airstream creates a [Bernoulli](/wiki/Bernoulli_effect "Bernoulli effect") or siphon. This excites the air contained in the [resonant cavity](/wiki/Resonant_cavity "Resonant cavity") (usually cylindrical) within the flute. The flutist changes the [pitch](/wiki/Pitch_(music) "Pitch (music)") of the sound produced by opening and closing holes in the body of the instrument, thus changing the effective length of the [resonator](/wiki/Resonator "Resonator") and its corresponding [resonant frequency](/wiki/Resonant_frequency "Resonant frequency"). By varying the air pressure, a flutist can also change the pitch by causing the air in the flute to resonate at a [harmonic](/wiki/Harmonic "Harmonic") rather than the [fundamental frequency](/wiki/Fundamental_frequency "Fundamental frequency") without opening or closing any of the holes.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

Head joint geometry appears particularly critical to acoustic performance and tone,[[45]](#cite_note-45) but there is no clear consensus among manufacturers on a particular shape. Acoustic impedance of the embouchure hole appears the most critical parameter.[[46]](#cite_note-46) Critical variables affecting this acoustic impedance include: the length of the chimney (the hole between the lip-plate and the head tube), chimney diameter, and radii or curvature of the ends of the chimney and any designed restriction in the "throat" of the instrument, such as that in the Japanese [Nohkan](/wiki/Nohkan "Nohkan") flute.

A study in which professional flutists were blindfolded could find no significant differences between flutes made from a variety of metals.[[47]](#cite_note-47) In two different sets of blind listening, no flute was correctly identified in a first listening, and in a second, only the silver flute was identified. The study concluded that there was "no evidence that the wall material has any appreciable effect on the sound color or dynamic range".

## Materials

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flute&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Materials")]

Historically, flutes were most commonly made of [reed](/wiki/Reed_(plant) "Reed (plant)"), bamboo, wood, or other organic materials. They were also made of glass, bone, and [nephrite](/wiki/Nephrite "Nephrite"). Most modern flutes are made of metal, primarily [silver](/wiki/Silver "Silver") and [nickel](/wiki/Nickel "Nickel"). Silver is less common than silver alloys.[[48]](#cite_note-48) Other materials used for flutes include gold, platinum, [grenadilla](/wiki/Dalbergia_melanoxylon "Dalbergia melanoxylon") and copper.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

## Types

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flute&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Types")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Zampo%C3%B1a.jpg/250px-Zampo%C3%B1a.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zampo%C3%B1a.jpg)

Playing the *[zampoña](/wiki/Siku_(panpipe) "Siku (panpipe)")*, a [Pre-Inca](/wiki/Cultural_periods_of_Peru "Cultural periods of Peru") instrument and type of [pan flute](/wiki/Pan_flute "Pan flute").

In its most basic form, a flute is an open tube which is blown into. After focused study and training, players use controlled air-direction to create an airstream in which the air is aimed downward into the tone hole of the flute's headjoint. There are several broad classes of flutes. With most flutes, the musician blows directly across the edge of the mouthpiece, with 1/4 of their bottom lip covering the embouchure hole. However, some flutes, such as the [whistle](/wiki/Whistle "Whistle"), [gemshorn](/wiki/Gemshorn "Gemshorn"), [flageolet](/wiki/Flageolet "Flageolet"), [recorder](/wiki/Recorder_(musical_instrument) "Recorder (musical instrument)"), [tin whistle](/wiki/Tin_whistle "Tin whistle"), [tonette](/wiki/Tonette "Tonette"), [fujara](/wiki/Fujara "Fujara"), and [ocarina](/wiki/Ocarina "Ocarina") have a duct that directs the air onto the edge (an arrangement that is termed a "[fipple](/wiki/Fipple "Fipple")"). These are known as **[fipple flutes](/wiki/Fipple_flutes "Fipple flutes")**. The fipple gives the instrument a distinct timbre which is different from non-fipple flutes and makes the instrument easier to play, but takes a degree of control away from the musician.

Another division is between side-blown (or [transverse](/wiki/Transverse_flute "Transverse flute")) flutes, such as the Western concert flute, [piccolo](/wiki/Piccolo "Piccolo"), [fife](/wiki/Fife_(musical_instrument) "Fife (musical instrument)"), [dizi](/wiki/Dizi_(instrument) "Dizi (instrument)") and [bansuri](/wiki/Bansuri "Bansuri"); and **[end-blown flutes](/wiki/End-blown_flute "End-blown flute")**, such as the [ney](/wiki/Ney "Ney"), [xiao](/wiki/Xiao_(flute) "Xiao (flute)"), [kaval](/wiki/Kaval "Kaval"), [danso](/wiki/Danso "Danso"), [shakuhachi](/wiki/Shakuhachi "Shakuhachi"), [Anasazi flute](/wiki/Anasazi_flute "Anasazi flute") and [quena](/wiki/Quena "Quena"). The player of a side-blown flute uses a hole on the side of the tube to produce a tone, instead of blowing on an end of the tube. End-blown flutes should not be confused with fipple flutes such as the [recorder](/wiki/Recorder_(musical_instrument) "Recorder (musical instrument)"), which are also played [vertically](/wiki/Vertical_flute "Vertical flute") but have an internal duct to direct the air flow across the edge of the tone hole.

Flutes may be open at one or both ends. The [ocarina](/wiki/Ocarina "Ocarina"), [xun](/wiki/Xun_(instrument) "Xun (instrument)"), [pan pipes](/wiki/Pan_pipes "Pan pipes"), [police whistle](/wiki/Police_whistle "Police whistle"), and [bosun's whistle](/wiki/Bosun%27s_whistle "Bosun's whistle") are closed-ended. Open-ended flutes such as the concert flute and the recorder have more harmonics, and thus more flexibility for the player, and brighter timbres. An organ pipe may be either open or closed, depending on the sound desired.

Flutes may have any number of pipes or tubes, though one is the most common number. Flutes with multiple [resonators](/wiki/Acoustic_resonance#Resonance_of_a_tube_of_air "Acoustic resonance") may be played one resonator at a time (as is typical with pan pipes) or more than one at a time (as is typical with double flutes).

Flutes can be played with several different air sources. Conventional flutes are blown with the mouth, although some cultures use [nose flutes](/wiki/Nose_flute "Nose flute"). The [flue pipes](/wiki/Flue_pipe "Flue pipe") of [organs](/wiki/Pipe_organ "Pipe organ"), which are acoustically similar to duct flutes, are blown by bellows or fans.

### Western transverse

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flute&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Western transverse")]

Main article: [Western concert flute](/wiki/Western_concert_flute "Western concert flute")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Flute_with_musicial_notes.jpg/330px-Flute_with_musicial_notes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Flute_with_musicial_notes.jpg)

[Western concert flute](/wiki/Western_concert_flute "Western concert flute")

#### Wooden one-keyed

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flute&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Wooden one-keyed")]

Usually starting from d1, wooden transverse flutes were played in European classical music mainly in the period from the early 18th century to the early 19th century. As such, the instrument is often indicated as [baroque flute](/wiki/Baroque_flute "Baroque flute"). Gradually marginalized by the Western concert flute in the 19th century, baroque flutes were again played from the late 20th century as part of the [historically informed performance](/wiki/Historically_informed_performance "Historically informed performance") practice.

#### Concert

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flute&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Concert")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Western_concert_flute.jpg/500px-Western_concert_flute.jpg)](/wiki/File:Western_concert_flute.jpg)

An illustration of a [Western concert flute](/wiki/Western_concert_flute "Western concert flute")

The [Western concert flute](/wiki/Western_concert_flute "Western concert flute"), a descendant of the medieval German flute, is a transverse treble flute that is closed at the top. An *[embouchure](/wiki/Embouchure "Embouchure") hole* is positioned near the top, and the flutist blows across it. The flute has circular tone holes larger than the finger holes of its baroque predecessors. The size and placement of tone holes, key mechanism, and fingering system used to produce the notes in the flute's [range](/wiki/Range_(music) "Range (music)") were evolved from 1832 to 1847 by [Theobald Boehm](/wiki/Theobald_Boehm "Theobald Boehm"), who helped greatly improve the instrument's dynamic range and intonation over its predecessors.[[50]](#cite_note-50) With some refinements (and the rare exception of the Kingma system and other custom adapted fingering systems), Western concert flutes typically conform to Boehm's design, known as the [Boehm system](/wiki/Boehm_system "Boehm system"). Beginner's flutes are made of nickel, silver, or brass that is silver-plated, while professionals use solid silver, gold, and sometimes even platinum flutes. There are also modern wooden-bodied flutes usually with silver or gold keywork. The wood is usually [African Blackwood](/wiki/African_Blackwood "African Blackwood").

The standard concert flute is pitched in C and has a range of three [octaves](/wiki/Octave "Octave") starting from [middle C](/wiki/Middle_C "Middle C") or one half step lower when a B foot is attached. This means that the concert flute is one of the highest-pitched common [orchestra](/wiki/Orchestra "Orchestra") and [concert band](/wiki/Concert_band "Concert band") instruments.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Piccolo_on_red_backdrop.jpg/250px-Piccolo_on_red_backdrop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Piccolo_on_red_backdrop.jpg)

Grenadilla wood piccolo with a modified wave headjoint

#### Concert variants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flute&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Concert variants")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Joueuse_de_fl%C3%BBte_%C3%A0_Ch%C3%A2teau-Thierry.jpg/250px-Joueuse_de_fl%C3%BBte_%C3%A0_Ch%C3%A2teau-Thierry.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joueuse_de_fl%C3%BBte_%C3%A0_Ch%C3%A2teau-Thierry.jpg)

Center: Piccolo. Right: larger flute

The [piccolo](/wiki/Piccolo "Piccolo") plays an octave higher than the regular treble flute. Lower members of the flute family include the [G alto](/wiki/Alto_flute "Alto flute") and [C bass](/wiki/Bass_flute "Bass flute") flutes that are used occasionally, and are pitched a perfect fourth and an octave below the concert flute, respectively. The [contra-alto](/wiki/Contra-alto_flute "Contra-alto flute"), [contrabass](/wiki/Contrabass_flute "Contrabass flute"), [subcontrabass](/wiki/Subcontrabass_flute "Subcontrabass flute"), [double contrabass](/wiki/Double_contrabass_flute "Double contrabass flute"), and [hyperbass](/wiki/Hyperbass_flute "Hyperbass flute") flutes are other rare forms of the flute pitched up to four octaves below middle C.

Other sizes of flutes and piccolos are used from time to time. A rarer instrument of the modern pitching system is the G [treble flute](/wiki/Treble_flute "Treble flute"). Instruments made according to an older pitch standard, used principally in wind-band music, include D♭ piccolo, E♭ [soprano flute](/wiki/Soprano_flute "Soprano flute") (Keyed a minor 3rd above the standard C flute), F alto flute, and B♭ bass flute.

### Indian

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flute&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Indian")]

Further information: [Bansuri](/wiki/Bansuri "Bansuri") and [Venu](/wiki/Venu "Venu")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Indian_bamboo_flute.jpg/250px-Indian_bamboo_flute.jpg)](/wiki/File:Indian_bamboo_flute.jpg)

A [Carnatic](/wiki/Carnatic_music "Carnatic music") eight-holed bamboo flute

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Eight_Flute1.JPG/250px-Eight_Flute1.JPG)](/wiki/File:Eight_Flute1.JPG)

An eight-holed classical Indian bamboo flute.

The [bamboo flute](/wiki/Bamboo_flute "Bamboo flute") is an important instrument in [Indian classical music](/wiki/Indian_classical_music "Indian classical music"), and developed independently of the Western flute. The [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") God Lord [Krishna](/wiki/Krishna "Krishna") is traditionally considered a master of the bamboo flute. The Indian flutes are very simple compared to the Western counterparts; they are made of [bamboo](/wiki/Bamboo "Bamboo") and are keyless.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

Two main varieties of Indian flutes are currently used. The first, the [Bansuri](/wiki/Bansuri "Bansuri") (बांसुरी), has six finger holes and one embouchure hole, and is used predominantly in the [Hindustani music](/wiki/Hindustani_music "Hindustani music") of Northern India. The second, the [Venu](/wiki/Venu "Venu") or Pullanguzhal, has eight finger holes, and is played predominantly in the [Carnatic music](/wiki/Carnatic_music "Carnatic music") of Southern India. Presently, the eight-holed flute with cross-fingering technique is common among many Carnatic flutists. Prior to this, the South Indian flute had only seven finger holes, with the fingering standard developed by Sharaba Shastri, of the Palladam school, at the beginning of the 20th century.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Cipriano_Garcia_playing_a_flute_of_the_Tohono_O%27odham_culture.jpg/250px-Cipriano_Garcia_playing_a_flute_of_the_Tohono_O%27odham_culture.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cipriano_Garcia_playing_a_flute_of_the_Tohono_O%27odham_culture.jpg)

Cipriano Garcia playing a flute of the [Tohono O'odham](/wiki/Tohono_O%CA%BCodham "Tohono Oʼodham") culture. Photograph by [Frances Densmore](/wiki/Frances_Densmore "Frances Densmore") taken in 1919.

The quality of the flute's sound depends somewhat on the specific [bamboo](/wiki/Bamboo "Bamboo") used to make it, and it is generally agreed that the best bamboo grows in the [Nagercoil](/wiki/Nagercoil "Nagercoil") area of South India.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

In 1998 [Bharata Natya Shastra](/w/index.php?title=Bharata_Natya_Shastra%27s&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bharata Natya Shastra's (page does not exist)") [Sarana Chatushtai](/wiki/Sarana_Chatushtai "Sarana Chatushtai"), [Avinash Balkrishna Patwardhan](/wiki/Avinash_Balkrishna_Patwardhan "Avinash Balkrishna Patwardhan") developed a methodology to produce perfectly tuned flutes for the ten 'thatas' currently present in Indian Classical Music.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

In a regional dialect of Gujarati, a flute is also called Pavo.[[55]](#cite_note-55) Some people can also play pair of flutes (Jodiyo Pavo) simultaneously.

### Chinese

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flute&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Chinese")]

Main article: [Chinese flutes](/wiki/Chinese_flutes "Chinese flutes")

In China there are many varieties of [dizi](/wiki/Dizi_(instrument) "Dizi (instrument)") (笛子), or Chinese flute, with different sizes, structures (with or without a resonance membrane) and number of holes (from 6 to 11) and intonations (different keys). Most are made of bamboo, but can come in wood, jade, bone, and iron. One peculiar feature of the Chinese flute is the use of a resonance membrane mounted on one of the holes that vibrates with the air column inside the tube. This membrane is called a *[di mo](/wiki/Di_mo "Di mo")*, which is usually a thin tissue paper. It gives the flute a bright sound.

Commonly seen flutes in the modern Chinese orchestra are the [bangdi](/wiki/Bangdi "Bangdi") (梆笛), [qudi](/wiki/Qudi "Qudi") (曲笛), [xindi](/wiki/Xindi_(instrument) "Xindi (instrument)") (新笛), and [dadi](/wiki/Dadi_(instrument) "Dadi (instrument)") (大笛). The bamboo flute played vertically is called the [xiao](/wiki/Xiao_(flute) "Xiao (flute)") (簫), which is a different category of wind instrument in China.

### Korean

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flute&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Korean")]

Main article: [Daegeum](/wiki/Daegeum "Daegeum")

The Korean flute, called the [daegeum](/wiki/Daegeum "Daegeum"), 대금, is a large bamboo transverse flute used in traditional Korean music. It has a buzzing membrane that gives it a unique timbre.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

### Japanese

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flute&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Japanese")]

Main article: [Fue (flute)](/wiki/Fue_(flute) "Fue (flute)")

The Japanese flute, called the [fue](/wiki/Fue_(flute) "Fue (flute)"), 笛 ([hiragana](/wiki/Hiragana "Hiragana"): ふえ), encompasses a large number of musical flutes from Japan, include the end-blown [shakuhachi](/wiki/Shakuhachi "Shakuhachi") and [hotchiku](/wiki/Hotchiku "Hotchiku"), as well as the transverse [gakubue](/w/index.php?title=Gakubue&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gakubue (page does not exist)"), [komabue](/wiki/Komabue "Komabue"), [ryūteki](/wiki/Ry%C5%ABteki "Ryūteki"), [nōkan](/wiki/N%C5%8Dkan "Nōkan"), [shinobue](/wiki/Shinobue "Shinobue"), [kagurabue](/wiki/Kagurabue "Kagurabue") and [minteki](/wiki/Minteki "Minteki").

### Sodina and suling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flute&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Sodina and suling")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Sodina_Flute_of_Madagascar.jpg/250px-Sodina_Flute_of_Madagascar.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sodina_Flute_of_Madagascar.jpg)

A [sodina](/wiki/Sodina "Sodina") player in Madagascar

The [sodina](/wiki/Sodina "Sodina") is an end-blown flute found throughout the island state of [Madagascar](/wiki/Madagascar "Madagascar"), located in the Indian Ocean off southeastern Africa. One of the oldest instruments on the island, it bears close resemblance to end-blown flutes found in Southeast Asia and particularly Indonesia, where it is known as the *[suling](/wiki/Suling "Suling")*, suggesting the predecessor to the sodina was carried to Madagascar in outrigger canoes by the island's original settlers emigrating from Borneo.[[56]](#cite_note-Musical_Standard-56) An image of the most celebrated contemporary sodina flutist, [Rakoto Frah](/wiki/Rakoto_Frah "Rakoto Frah") (d. 2001), was featured on the [local currency](/wiki/Ariary "Ariary").[[57]](#cite_note-57)

### Sring

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flute&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Sring")]

The [sring](/wiki/Sring "Sring") (also called *blul*) is a relatively small, end-blown flute with a nasal tone quality[[58]](#cite_note-Pahlevanian_2001-58) found in the Caucasus region of Eastern Armenia. It is made of wood or cane, usually with seven finger holes and one thumb hole,[[58]](#cite_note-Pahlevanian_2001-58) producing a diatonic scale. One Armenian musicologist believes the sring to be the most characteristic of national Armenian instruments.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

### Ọjà

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flute&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Ọjà")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Oja.jpg/250px-Oja.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oja.jpg)

The Ọjà, which is a traditional Igbo flute

The [Ọjà](/wiki/%E1%BB%8Cj%C3%A0 "Ọjà") [//](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q33578_(ibo)-Olugold-%E1%BB%8Cj%C3%A0.wav "File:LL-Q33578 (ibo)-Olugold-Ọjà.wav") is a traditional musical instrument utilized by the [Igbo people](/wiki/Igbo_people "Igbo people"), who are indigenous to [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria"). The ọjà (flute) is used during cultural activities or events where [Igbo music](/wiki/Igbo_music "Igbo music") is played. It is skillfully carved from wood/bamboo or metal and is played by blowing air into one end while covering and uncovering holes along the body to create different notes.[[60]](#cite_note-:1-60)

## Breathing techniques

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flute&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Breathing techniques")]

There are several means by which flautists breathe to blow air through the instrument and produce sound. They include [diaphragmatic breathing](/wiki/Diaphragmatic_breathing "Diaphragmatic breathing") and [circular breathing](/wiki/Circular_breathing "Circular breathing"). Diaphragmatic breathing optimizes inhalation, minimizing the number of breaths. Circular breathing brings air in through the nose and out through the mouth, enabling a continuous sound.
